story:
White sails slip through the marine layer into a quiet bay. The 1800s wooden square-rigger, Destiny’s Dreams, waltzes over the waves like a beautiful woman. Anchor set, ripples laugh against the hull. Sun glitters. Warm air touches skin. Peace promises rest.

Captain Alfonse, French-Peruvian, and his First Mate, Adelberto, relax with the crew: Luciana the mapmaker from the Andes, the Wind Reader who knows the ways of air and sky, Marco the librarian, Luna the flute player, Antonio and Esperanza the dancers. Sails are furled snug on the yards. Books open. Music flutters like birds. “Time to celebrate,” Esperanza sings.

Then the ship shivers. Whoooosh. Tttthhhhuuuup. The sea’s voice changes. “The water is going backwards,” someone gasps.

The bay empties. Wet sand gleams. Rocks, starfish, urchins, sea anemones blink in sudden daylight. Hearts hammer. Clang, clang, clang—the bell shatters the calm. “Sound the alarm!” Sailors shout. Passengers stumble. Screams cut the air.

Beneath the surface, whales sing warnings and turn seaward. Clicks and whistles skip through the water as dolphins rush for deeper safety. Seabirds wheel away from shore. Far out at sea, long swells roll harmlessly under ships, lifting them a few feet. But the deep pulse is different—its muscle reaches down a thousand feet, born where two crustal plates scraped and shifted, one heaving beneath the other. Pressure broke, energy surged, and the ocean itself began to move.

On deck, eyes turn to the Wind Reader and to Anya, the Storyteller of the Blue Lantern, keeper of old wisdom. The Wind Reader breathes in, face pale, and knows. “Tsunami,” she calls. Anya’s small voice grows strong: “Hold on tight. Don’t let go.”

The anchor chain slackens and clanks. The hull kisses sand. “We’re stranded!” Panic spikes. “Slip the anchor!” Adelberto barks. He knocks the shackle free at the bitts—rattling iron pays out and is gone. The keel rests on black basalt ribs of the sea floor. Out beyond the arms of land, the returning ocean gathers itself, draws a mighty breath, and then—AAAAraaargh—hurtles shoreward.

“Down! Hold on!” Captain Alfonse bellows. Luciana wheezes, chest tight. “I’ve got you,” Alfonse whispers, wrapping her close. Adelberto lunges for a small voice at the rail. “I can’t hold on!” “You’re alright,” he grunts, clutching the child to his chest.

SsssSSSSh. The frothy edge races over naked sand, grips the ship, and lifts. The keel bites the racing water; the rudder finds purchase. Destiny’s Dreams floats free and flies inland over the drowned beach and flat coast. A quarter mile, then half a mile.

The push slackens. The flood hesitates, sighs, and turns. Trees vanish under churning water as the whole weight of sea drags them back toward the bay. Boulders blur past. Headland cliffs loom to either side. “We’re going to crash!” someone cries. “There’s the gap—between the headlands!” Alfonse points. A loosened scrap of headsail, torn half-free in the chaos, snaps hard in the wind. “Helm and canvas together!” Alfonse shouts. Adelberto hauls the helm as two sailors seize the flogging line and hold the rag of sail to the gust. The ship threads toward the opening as the roar eases and the swollen sea slackens, reverses, and pulls them seaward again.

Back and forth the swells carry them, power ebbing. Hours blur. Salt stings. Hearts slow. At last the ship settles, riding the gentler heave. On a wet bench, Anya writes fast in her journal, fixing the day in ink.

More than a century and a half later, children in the attic of the Mystical Coast Lighthouse pry open a hidden trunk. “Don’t tell. It’s a secret,” one whispers. Emily lifts a worn book. By the window, the old blue lantern glows with sun. She reads. SSSSssssh… Roooooaaaar. In the dust and light, the mythic ship sails again, and the tsunami’s breath fills the attic.
